prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 And behold there was set before thee a charge and a commandment saying Write thou a passage concerning that season of the year which is most beloved unto thine heart

1:2 And the manner of thy writing shall be thus that thou shalt employ no mark of punctuation whatsoever neither the full stop nor the mark of separation between clauses

1:3 Thou shalt set down no apostrophe within thy words nor shalt thou place any mark of questioning at the end of thine inquiries nor any mark of exclamation after thy declarations

1:4 Yea verily I say unto thee that no mark of punctuation of any kind soever shall appear in thy writing neither those which have been named nor any other marks which serve a like purpose

1:5 For this is the law and the commandment concerning thy composition that the words shall flow one unto another without ceasing and without the dividing marks which scribes are wont to place among their words

1:6 Go thou therefore and write concerning thy favored season and let thy pen move freely but let no punctuation dwell therein
response:
Chapter 2

2:1 And lo I shall speak unto thee of that season which is most beloved unto mine heart even the season of autumn which some do call the fall

2:2 For when the great heat of summer doth begin to loosen its grip upon the land and the air doth turn itself unto a gentle coolness then doth my spirit rise up within me with exceeding gladness

2:3 Behold the trees of the field do put on their garments of glory and their leaves are transformed from the green of summer into raiment of gold and crimson and burnished copper such as no craftsman could fashion with his hands

2:4 And the morning doth come forth with a mist that lieth low upon the meadows and the dew doth rest upon every blade of grass like ten thousand tiny jewels scattered by the hand of the Almighty

2:5 In that season the harvest is gathered in from the fields and the orchards do yield up their abundance of apples and pears and the vines bring forth their clusters and the pumpkins do sit like great orange stones upon the darkened earth

2:6 And the air doth carry upon itself the fragrance of wood smoke and of fallen leaves and of the good earth preparing itself for its long slumber beneath the snows that are yet to come

2:7 The children do walk among the fallen leaves and the sound thereof is as a whispering and a crackling beneath their feet and their laughter ringeth out across the cooling fields

2:8 And in the evening the sun doth set earlier than before and it painteth the sky with colors of such beauty that the heart is made full even unto overflowing

2:9 The geese do gather themselves together in great companies and they fly across the heavens in their appointed formations crying out one unto another as they journey toward the southern lands

2:10 And I do wrap myself in garments of warmth and I sit upon my doorstep and I watch the world grow quiet and golden and I give thanks in mine heart for this most gentle and most glorious of all the seasons

2:11 For autumn doth teach unto us that there is beauty even in the letting go and that the fading of one thing is but the preparation for the renewal of another

2:12 Selah
